title: Roscón de Reyes
subtitle: Spanish Three Kings ring cake with candied fruit
excerpt: Roscón de Reyes is the Spanish celebration bread for Epiphany on the 6th of January: a large, ring-shaped enriched yeast bread fragrant with orange blossom water and citrus, decorated with strips of candied fruit across the top. The dough is brioche-like (butter, eggs, and milk enriched) and bakes golden and soft. It is sliced at the table, and tradition holds that a small figurine or dried bean is hidden inside: whoever finds it provides next year's roscón.
sourceNotes: Roscón de Reyes has been made in Spain since the medieval period, when a gold coin was the traditional hidden prize. The ring shape is said to represent the crown of the Magi. Recipes appear in Juan de la Mata's 'Arte de Repostería' (1747), one of the earliest Spanish confectionery manuals. The orange blossom water is an Andalusian influence dating from the Moorish period.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze undecorated. Apply the candied fruit after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can prove overnight in the fridge after shaping. Decorate and bake from cold, adding 5 minutes to the bake time.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flavouring distilled from the flowers of the bitter orange tree. Use sparingly — a tablespoon in a large bread dough gives a delicate floral fragrance; more quickly becomes soapy. Common in Spanish, North African, and Levantine baking. Keeps indefinitely stored in a cool, dark place. Rose water and orange blossom water are not interchangeable in flavour.

content.text: The dough is enriched with butter, eggs, and milk, so it develops a soft, slightly sweet crumb close to brioche.

and citrus zest are what distinguish this from a plain sweet bun. The ring is formed by rolling the dough into a long rope and joining the ends around a foil ball or small oven-safe bowl placed in the centre to keep the hole open during baking.

Combine 500 g flour, 7 g yeast, 80 g sugar, and 5 g salt. Mix in 120 ml warm milk, 3 beaten eggs, 1 tbsp orange blossom water, and the zests of 1 orange and 1 lemon. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth, then incorporate 80 g softened butter a little at a time, kneading well after each addition. The dough will be soft and slightly tacky. Cover and leave at room temperature for 75 minutes until doubled.

Line a large baking tray. Knock back the dough and roll into a rope about 60 cm long. Place a ball of foil (about 6 cm across) in the centre of the tray. Form the rope into a ring around it, pinching the ends firmly together. Cover and prove for 40 to 45 minutes until puffed.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Brush the ring with beaten egg. Arrange 80 g mixed peel in strips across the top, scatter 30 g flaked almonds, and sprinkle with 2 tbsp caster sugar. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until golden. Cool on a rack for 20 minutes before serving. Remove the foil ball.

items.problem: Ring closed up during baking and lost its hole
items.solution: The foil ball was too small, or removed before baking. Keep it in throughout the bake; it will pull out cleanly once the bread is cool and set.

items.problem: Candied fruit sank into the dough during baking
items.solution: The decoration should only be applied after the egg wash, just before the ring goes into the oven. Never press the peel into unproved dough — it sinks as the bread bakes around it.

items.problem: Dough is very stiff and did not rise well
items.solution: The butter was added too early before the gluten had developed, or the milk was too hot and impaired the yeast. Add butter only after the initial kneading, and check the milk is no more than 35 degrees C.
